attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of persistent red, scaly patches on her face. The lesions are painless but occasionally itchy and appear to worsen after sun exposure. She has no other symptoms and feels otherwise well. Her sister has Hashimoto's disease and her father has type 1 diabetes mellitus. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as an outdoor swimming instructor.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with adherent scale on her cheeks and nose. The centre of the lesions shows atrophy with loss of pigmentation. There are no oral ulcers, joint swelling, or other skin lesions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would confirm the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (CLE), including well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with adherent scale and central atrophy over the cheeks and scalp in a 'butterfly rash' presentation. The photosensitive nature of the lesions and their distribution in sun-exposed areas are typical features. It can also cause alopecia if over hairy areas. Lupus erythematous is a chronic autoimmune disease and the cause is unclear. It typically presents in patients in their 20 - 50s. It presents as acute CLE, suabacute CLE and chronic CLE. This patient presents with features typical of discoid cutaneous lupus erythematosus (DLE) specifically which is a form of chronic CLE.

Skin biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of CLE. Histopathology typically shows interface dermatitis, follicular plugging, and basement membrane thickening. The biopsy should include both lesional and perilesional skin for optimal diagnostic yield. As treatment can require systemic management, diagnosis is important.

Anti-nuclear antibody testing is not the most appropriate first step as it may be negative in isolated CLE. While positive ANA may suggest systemic lupus erythematosus, the diagnosis of CLE is primarily based on clinical features and histopathology. This test would likely be done alongside investigation for the patient however skin biopsy is gold standard for diagnosis of CLE.

Patch testing is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis. While contact dermatitis can present with erythematous plaques, it typically does not show the characteristic scarring and atrophy seen in this patient. It is also not triggered by sunlight as in this case.

Direct immunofluorescence of normal skin (DIF) can be helpful in diagnosing systemic lupus erythematosus but is not the most appropriate first-line investigation for suspected CLE. When needed, DIF should be performed on lesional skin.

Fungal scraping and culture would be appropriate if tinea was suspected. However, the clinical presentation with photosensitivity, scarring, and atrophy is not consistent with a fungal infection.
learningPoints: Discoid lupus erythematosus typically presents with well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with adherent scale and central atrophy.
Sun exposure is a common trigger for cutaneous lupus erythematosus lesions.
Biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of cutaneous lupus erythematosus.
references: Grönhagen CM, Nyberg F. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus: An update. Indian Dermatol Online J. 2014 Jan;5(1):7-13.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3937495/
Saracino A.  Cutaneous lupus erythematosus.  The Australasian College of Dermatologists.  2024. Available at: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/cutaneous-lupus-erythematosus/
